title: Tour de France rivals Pogacar, Vingegaard given night-time doping controls
description: Tour de France leader Tadej Pogacar and his main rival Jonas Vingegaard both confirmed on Sunday that they were visited in the middle of the night by anti-doping controllers, just hours before a crucial mountain stage.The night-time doping control came just hours before the start of a 183.9km stage with barely a metre of flat roads including four categorised climbs.
fullText:
Tour de France leader Tadej Pogacar and his main rival Jonas Vingegaard both confirmed on Sunday that they were visited in the middle of the night by anti-doping controllers, just hours before a crucial mountain stage.

French newspaper Le Parisien had said that both four-time winner Pogacar and two-time former champion Vingegaard, were targeted by International Testing Agency (ITA) officials at their hotels during the night.

"I got four hours of sleep today. Not (at) 2:00 am in the morning, because they were (there at) 5:00 in the morning," he said before the start of Sunday's 15th stage from Champagnole in the Jura to Plateau de Solaison in the Alps when asked about the report.

"But I always go to sleep late. It was a big shock for me also to get four hours of sleep today.

"But I had a very cool long morning. I couldn't fall back to sleep, so I was chilling, listening to some old Eminem hits, and making coffees, so I over-caffeinated.

"I was ready to start the day, but it was definitely not nice to wake up in the middle of sweet dreams."

Pogacar has spent more than 10 days in the leader's yellow jersey at the Tour, meaning that he has been tested at the end of each of those stages, including on Saturday.

"And also, they come randomly in the mornings through the Tour," he added.

"And today they chose nighttime."

Vingegaard's control came a few hours before Pogacar's, but likewise woke him from his sleep.

"I guess the ITA didn't allow me to sleep very much. I was sleeping very well but then somebody knocked on my door at 2:00 am," Vingegaard told reporters.

"(It was) a big surprise to me. Of course, it's good that they test but when it's affecting performance or your sleep, I don't think it's good."

Vingegaard was partially just concerned that he had not been the only rider targeted.

"It has to be equal for everybody, but it's not the nicest at two o'clock," he added.

"It took me about 40 minutes before I was back in my room.

"I still feel OK but it was not the best night. I cannot change it."

Richard Plugge, the managing director of Vingegaard's Visma-Lease a Bike team, said that other teams had also been targeted in the night, without giving details.

"It is good that our sport is, and remains, so on top of the anti-doping fight," Plugge told Dutch cycling news platform Wielerflits, admitting that it was "unpleasant" to face such night-time controls.

The night-time doping control came just hours before the start of a 183.9km stage with barely a metre of flat roads including four categorised climbs.

The stage finishes on the brutally tough 11km-long Plateau de Solaison, which has an average gradient of nine percent.

Slovenian Pogacar currently leads Vingegaard in the overall standings by four and a half minutes, while another five riders are within 1min 20sec of the Dane.

While the two Tour favourites seemed to take it in their stride, Vingegaard's American team-mate Matteo Jorgenson was livid.

"I was asked to keep my opinion on this to myself, but this is a complete lack of respect for the riders," he told reporters.

"We are focusing heavily on recovery. That is hugely important, especially between two of the toughest mountain stages in this Tour.

"And then you get woken up at 2:00 am. That makes me really furious."

title: Spurs' CEO Breaks Silence on LeBron James Rumors
description: The San Antonio Spurs continue to be linked to LeBron James in free agency, and one of their major decision-makers offered a hint as to how the Spurs want to proceed.
fullText:
The San Antonio Spurs had the means this offseason to make a splashy, league-altering move. Instead, they opted to make two first-round draft picks and sign Tobias Harris. All other contracts were re-signing or extending existing players, like Victor Wembanyama.

Of course, Dylan Harper and Stephon Castle are on their rookie-scale contracts, but won't be for long. As the Spurs plan for the future, keeping some flexibility open needs to be a top priority. It's why they didn't sign players looking for long-term deals.

But LeBron James wouldn't want a long-term deal. The Spurs have been linked to him, although teams like Cleveland, Miami, Minnesota, and Philadelphia all seem more reasonable.

On the court, James is a clear fit for the Spurs. Off the court, the Spurs just don't seem to be trying very hard.

The Spurs, by and large, do not make blockbuster moves. Signing James to even a one-year, minimum deal would make waves, and the Spurs generally don't do that.

San Antonio entered the offseason with a clear need at the power forward position, but instead of adding a player of James' caliber, it settled for Harris. The former Piston is capable, but he isn't exactly a superstar.

If you'd listen to the Spurs, that's exaclty what they wantred.

“What I will say is that we’re excited about the team we’ve been able to keep together, and to add Tobias to that, plus our young draft picks," Spurs' CEO RC Buford told L'Équipe's Maxime Aubin. "I think our history shows how we build teams.”

But is that all smoke and mirrors? After all, James wouldn't impact the Spurs' long-term plans, but he would make the roster much, much better next season. A starting five of Castle, Wembanyama, James, De'Aaron Fox, and Devin Vassell, with Harper, Harris, Julian Champagnie, Keldon Johnson, Jayden Quaintance, Tarris Reed, and Harrison Barnes coming off the bench, would be one of the best in the league.

The Spurs are known as a team that doesn't make flashy moves, but even more than that, they're a team that plays things close to the chest. Take Buford's comments with a grain of salt; the Spurs are not a team to make a public pursuit.

title: Juventus urged to sign one specific player after first summer friendly
description: Juventus were held to a 0-0 draw by Basel in their opening pre-season friendly, but the bigger concern was Jeff Ekhator’s muscle injury, with the Italian media now urging the Bianconeri to sign a ne...
fullText:
Juventus were held to a 0-0 draw by Basel in their opening pre-season friendly, but the bigger concern was Jeff Ekhator’s muscle injury, with the Italian media now urging the Bianconeri to sign a new centre-forward.

Juventus’ goalless draw against Stephan Lichtsteiner’s Basel on Saturday, paired with Ekhator’s injury after just 29 minutes have made the Bianconeri’s need for a new centre-forward even more evident.

Gazzetta dello Sport and Tuttosport have similar headlines on Sunday as both Italian sports papers urge the Bianconeri to sign a new attacker this summer, particularly PSG’s Randal Kolo Muani.

It is no secret that signing a goalkeeper and, at least, one centre-forward is among Juventus’ priorities for the current transfer window.

It is also no secret that Kolo Muani is the Old Lady’s big target up front, yet negotiations with PSG have proved to be tricky.

During their 0-0 draw against Basel, Mattia Perin saved a penalty, and Fabio Miretti missed a big opportunity in front of the Basel keeper, while summer signing Ekhator suffered a muscle injury in the first half.

Jonathan David and Lois Openda are not believed to be part of Luciano Spalletti’s plans, so, besides Kolo Muani, the Bianconeri have also opened talks with Parma’s Mateo Pellegrino and enquired about Tottenham’s Richarlison.

Their ex-striker Dusan Vlahovic remains available as a free agent, and Spalletti has recently opened the door to his return, as long as the striker lowers his salary demands.

title: Pressley 'happy with exercise' despite 4-0 loss
description: Manager Steven Pressley was "happy with the exercise" despite Dundee losing 4-0 at home to Everton in Saturday's friendly at Dens Park.  Pressley's Scottish Premiership side trailed 1-0 at the break and lost three more goals to the Premier League outfit in the second period.  "It was very important and we got some really good minutes into the likes of Zan Besir," Pressley told club media.
fullText:
Manager Steven Pressley was "happy with the exercise" despite Dundee losing 4-0 at home to Everton in Saturday's friendly at Dens Park.

Pressley's Scottish Premiership side trailed 1-0 at the break and lost three more goals to the Premier League outfit in the second period.

"It was very important and we got some really good minutes into the likes of Zan Besir," Pressley told club media. "We got 60 minutes into him, Joe Westley 60 minutes. We got 45 minutes into [Alan] Forrest and of course additional minutes to a number of players.

"Also we welcome back Fin [Robertson] after a setback with an injury.

"So, it was good in that respect and I was really satisfied with a lot of things, especially in the first half where I suppose we had players that have been with us for a longer period that understand the principles more and I thought we were very aggressive.

"The second half, we worked really hard. We were just out of sync a little bit in certain things but that's normal with certain players playing out of position. We also had new players in the team.

"Overall, I was really, really happy with the exercise."

Dundee continue their Premier Sports Cup group campaign away to Ross County on Tuesday (19:45 BST) and finish at home to Clyde on Saturday (15:00). Pressley's side have maximum points from their first two group fixtures.

title: Lewiston siblings Kobe, Katy Wessels shining from the track to the court
description: Jul. 19—Siblings Kobe and Katy Wessels couldn't sit still while growing up in Lewiston. So, their parents, Stephanie and Chad Wessels, put them in every sport or activity imaginable. "We played soccer, track, basketball," Katy Wessels said. "We did everything.
fullText:
Jul. 19—Siblings Kobe and Katy Wessels couldn't sit still while growing up in Lewiston.

So, their parents, Stephanie and Chad Wessels, put them in every sport or activity imaginable.

"We played soccer, track, basketball," Katy Wessels said. "We did everything."

That hyperactivity set them on a path to collegiate sports careers. Kobe, a four-time All-American, just wrapped up his four-year NAIA cross country/track and field career at Lewis-Clark State College, while Katy Wessels enters her fourth year of NCAA Division I volleyball at the University of Washington.

"It's just a mentality," LC State cross country/track and field coach Mike Collins said. "You need to build off of each other, and I think that's why you see athletes from certain families and certain groups do better."

While most of his friends embraced football, basketball and baseball, Kobe Wessels' path was distinct.

He had gone on numerous fun runs with his mother growing up, and gravitated to distance running in middle and high school.

In terms of "fitting in," those sports didn't do him many favors, Kobe said.

But what they did give him was a purpose, a community, and some of the deepest friendships and relationships in his life.

"It was kind of hard to take it super seriously until I just eventually loved the sport and continued to try my best," Kobe said.

The summer before his senior year at Lewiston High, Kobe began dating Emily Collins — a fellow Bengal track standout and the daughter of LCSC track and cross country coach Mike Collins.

Kobe spent time with Emily's family over the next year and it was at the Collins household when Mike Collins essentially recruited Kobe at his dinner table.

"'Hey, are you planning on running (in college)?'" Mike Collins said he asked Kobe, to which he responded that he didn't know. "It's like 'Well, I'd like you to run for me,' and it is kind of funny, because I'm not sure he really thought about it a whole lot."

Not only had Kobe not seriously considered a college athletics career prior to this conversation, he wasn't even planning on going to college, Kobe said.

It wasn't because he didn't want to, though, as Emily Collins shortly learned.

"My dad went inside, and that was it. And so I turned to Kobe, and I said, 'Do you want to run in college? Like, do you want to run at LC?' and he was like, 'Oh yeah, I do, like, that would be cool, I would totally do that.' And I said, 'Okay, well, when he asks you again, you say yes,"' Emily Collins said. "So I told my dad, I was like, 'Ask him again another time, and he will probably give you a more accurate answer than that.'"

Sure enough, Mike Collins asked again, and Kobe became a Warrior.

At LC, Kobe exhibited exponential growth, gradually implementing his coaches' feedback into his craft, all the way to numerous All-American marks.

"He doesn't get super high, he doesn't get super low, he takes things in stride, and he's definitely very coachable," Mike Collins said of Kobe. "He listens, he pays attention, and he learns as he goes."

That dedication, Collins said, has grown Kobe's confidence to the point where he earned All-America honors as part of the outdoor 3,200-meter relay team his freshman and sophomore seasons and an All-American finish in cross country during that second year.

However, sickness derailed his All-American pursuit his junior year, setting the stage for an epic finish in 2026.

Kobe claimed All-American honors in the 1,000 at the 2026 NAIA Indoor National Championships in March, starting his kick about 300 meters out and finishing in third place.

"I remember walking up thinking I was ready," Kobe said. "I don't remember much else after that."

At the finish line Emily was waiting for him.

"He walked over and I gave him a hug and he was like, 'I'm sweaty,' and I was like, 'Yeah, I can tell.'" Emily Collins said, her voice brimming with pride.

Kobe's career has been amazing to watch, Katy said, and inspired her to work even harder.

"He has accomplished great things, and he is definitely one of the hardest workers I know," Katy said. "Watching him grow from the high school athlete that he was to becoming a four-time All-American was just insane. He's worked so hard."

Ask her brother, and Katy Wessels is one of the best Lewiston Bengal athletes in school history.

Her accolades back it up, earning all-league honors in basketball, winning four letters in track and field and earning three volleyball all-league awards.

Asked if she agreed with her brother's assessment, Katy took a humble approach.

"I would say growing up in Lewiston and going to Lewiston High School, I think it just inspired me to work hard," Katy said.

Part way through her high school career, Katy said that she realized that volleyball was her best sport and ticket to the next level.

So, she sunset her basketball days in favor of club volleyball, commuting up to the Spokane area multiple times per week.

Her club teams T3 (2022) and CPA (2022-23), expanded her skills and in 2023 she helped CPA win an Evergreen Region U18 title.

"Being a part of that showed me that there were other girls out there that had the same dream as me of playing collegiate sports at a very high level," Katy said.

Eventually, colleges took notice of Katy, including one of the biggest institutions in the region — the University of Washington.

UW's head volleyball coach Leslie Gabriel — known to her players by an abbreviation of her maiden name, Tui — made a trip to Coeur d'Alene to watch Katy play.

Katy said she recalls being intimidated to meet Gabriel for the first time, but quickly came to know who she truly was.

"I moved away at 17 years old to go to UW to go play, and I was getting really homesick, and I had told Tui, then Tui took me out to ice cream," Katy said. "From then on I almost look at her as like a mother figure that I have in Seattle."

Gabriel's inaugural season at UW was Katy's freshman year, so Katy has seen the program grow through its first two seasons in the Big Ten Conference and first three years of Gabriel's team building.

Katy is one of the older players on the roster now, giving her a newfound leadership role.

"I'm not (a) 'gonna-give-you-a-whole-spiel' kind of leader," Katy said. "I want everyone to think of the game in their own way. ... I want to be the hardest worker, and since I'm surrounded by people that also want to be the hardest workers, that's how we push each other."

These days, you're apt to find Kobe Wessels traversing Highway 95 and U.S. 195 between Lewiston and Pullman.

The recent college grad has a full-time gig at Schweitzer Engineering Laboratories as part of the company's research and development team.

Thanks to a recent amendment to the NCAA's rules that extends athletes' eligibility to a full five years, Katy can play two more seasons of college volleyball, instead of just one.

"I was definitely not ready to be over with college," she said. "So I'm very excited that I have two more years."

That's two more years of memories with her teammates and two more years of the Wessels clan traveling near and far to support her.

As for Kobe, running will continue to be a part of his life in some form, he said.

It's also likely that a number of Seattle trips are in his future.

A staple of the Wessels clan. Showing up.

Last fall, when UW visited Pullman for a showdown against Washington State, Katy enjoyed a packed house of Wessels' family and friends at Bohler Gym.

That was far from the only match her parents attended, however. The two made frequent trips to Seattle and across the country to support their daughter. They also showed up for their son.

"My parents have missed very few of my meets. That's pretty impressive because I've had a lot of them. Same with Katy's games; I mean, they're traveling all over the place, working just as hard as we are to give us the opportunity," Kobe said of his parents. "Family comes first."

Taylor can be reached at 208-848-2260, staylor@lmtribune.com, or on X or Instagram @Sam_C_Taylor.

title: Kwity Paye donates school supplies to 100 children in Providence
description: Paye hosted 100 children for an afternoon of back-to-school activities that included distribution of supplies, backpacks, water bottles and laptops.
fullText:
PROVIDENCE – The business of football will resume soon enough. Saturday was personal for Kwity Paye.

The former standout at Bishop Hendricken was back in his home city for an event with his Paye Family Foundation. Paye hosted upwards of 100 kids at Providence Place Mall for an afternoon of back-to-school activities that included distribution of supplies, backpacks, water bottles and laptops.

Paye walked through the double doors of a private function room at Dave & Buster’s to a warm welcome. Children and family members had already arrived from the Providence Church of Christ and were digging into an early lunch. Paye also sponsored free video games and activities on the arcade floor following the more formal portion of the event.

“It’s nice being able to do it for my people, for my church, all my family members,” Paye said. “It’s just something that I always wanted to do my whole life – give back to my community. These people raised me.”

Paye’s family fled civil war in Liberia before he was born in a refugee camp in Guinea. His mother, Agnes, left West Africa for Providence with multiple family members when Paye was just an infant. Paye lived in the city through his college years, exploding to stardom in multiple sports with the Hawks and eventually accepting a scholarship offer from Michigan.

“I always wanted to do a foundation,” Paye said. “That was always my goal and my dream. It took a little while – I've got the right group around me now.”

Paye received postseason recognition twice from the Big Ten during his four years with the Wolverines. He was a first-round NFL Draft pick by the Indianapolis Colts in 2021, tabbed by a team that had reached the playoffs in two of the previous three years. Paye totaled 30.5 sacks and 37 tackles for loss in 75 games with the Colts, a tenure as an edge rusher that spanned five years.

“I want to be that beacon of hope, of inspiration,” Paye said. “It’s just showing the kids it’s possible – not just in athletics. Education-wise they need the support.”

Paye entered free agency for the first time in the offseason as a 27-year-old after reaching the end of his rookie contract with Indianapolis. He agreed to a three-year, $48-million deal with the Las Vegas Raiders that included more than $31 million in guaranteed money and more than $10 million in signing bonus. Paye is part of a significant incoming class for the Raiders that includes quarterback Kirk Cousins, center Tyler Linderbaum and linebackers Quay Walker and Nakobe Dean.

“I knew I was going to land somewhere,” Paye said. “It was more the best fit for me in my career going into the best years of my career. You don’t want it to be wasted with someone.”

Paye shares a pair of connections with one member of the current ownership group in Las Vegas. Tom Brady is a fellow Michigan graduate and helped shape Paye’s childhood fandom while capturing six Super Bowl titles with the New England Patriots. Brady’s presence with the Raiders helped lend some credibility to their offseason pursuits over the past few months.

“I grew up rooting for Tom Brady and his success being a New England guy,” Paye said. “Now I’m playing for a team that he partly owns – full-circle moment. You can see his influence this year.”

Paye’s foundation is geared toward helping students and young athletes in the communities where he's lived and played, and Saturday’s event was held in conjunction with Legacy Philanthropy. This type of support helped put him on the field in the city and get him to Bishop Hendricken, where his No. 1 was retired in June 2024. Paye's professional and charitable work follows the example of fellow Hawks alum Will Blackmon, a two-time Super Bowl winner who played 10 seasons in the NFL from 2006-16.

This article originally appeared on The Providence Journal: Kwity Paye donates school supplies to 100 children in Providence

title: Tom Heaton predicts Senne Lammens’ reaction to World Cup mistake
description: Manchester United veteran Tom Heaton has thrown his support behind Senne Lammens after the 23-year-old Belgian’s mistake at the World Cup, revealing how he will react to it next season.World Cup Hea...
fullText:
Manchester United veteran Tom Heaton has thrown his support behind Senne Lammens after the 23-year-old Belgian’s mistake at the World Cup, revealing how he will react to it next season.

After a superb debut season at Old Trafford following his £18.1 million move from Royal Antwerp last year, Lammens was selected by Rudi Garcia as part of his squad for this summer’s tournament in North America.

Real Madrid superstar Thibaut Courtois is the undisputed starter for Belgium, having made 115 appearances for his country.

Lammens was entrusted with the No. 2 role for the Red Devils, while Chelsea’s Mike Penders was the third choice. This constituted a remarkable rise for the United man, given he only made his first appearance for Belgium in November.

However, delight turned to disaster when Garcia summoned him from the bench in the quarter-final clash with Spain, after Courtois went down with a thigh injury in the 71st minute. Lammens entered the pitch with the game – his debut at a major tournament – poised at 1-1, with Charles De Ketelaere’s header having cancelled out Fabian Ruiz’s opener.

But as the clock ticked past the 88th minute, Lammens made a mess of a speculative shot from distance by Pau Cubarsí, spilling the ball into the path of the onrushing Mikel Merino, who converted to send Spain through to the semi-finals.

Naturally, Lammens was devastated at the final whistle, with Courtois making a beeline to console his compatriot, as did Spanish No. 1 Unai Simon to show the goalkeeping brotherhood extends beyond borders.

In a post-match interview, the Madrid shot stopper backed Lammens, stating: “You only get stronger from this. Eventually you cannot say much more to him or give him much more advice. He is a strong guy, strong personality. I’m sure he’ll be fine. You know, he will have some holidays and then regroup in Manchester and have a great season.”

This view is mirrored by his teammate at Old Trafford, with Heaton confirming he had spoken to Lammens after the quarter-final, but predicting he will “be stronger” for the experience, as tough as it is in the moment.

“It was obviously a challenging moment for him, but I think that’s the life of a goalkeeper. As soon as you put the gloves on and go between the sticks, you have to be ready to deal with that. He certainly is,” the 40-year-old stated.

“I actually think he’ll be stronger for it. His personality is fantastic for a goalkeeper, so no issues there. It’s obviously disappointing for him, but as I say, I do honestly think it can help him in the long run. It’s certainly tough but his personality is suited for it. He’s very strong mentally and he’ll be absolutely fine.”

When asked whether it was the type of high-profile mistake which can haunt a goalkeeper in the future, Heaton was confident it would not.

“That can happen at any point. Literally every time you cross the foot over the earth, so if that’s your mindset and that’s your outlook, then I’d be more inclined to say it’s more likely to happen.

“It’s accepting the fact that it can happen, but what you want to try and do and go and embrace it? I’ve certainly seen that from him as a young goalkeeper coming into Manchester United and dealing with that unbelievably well.

“I highly doubt he’ll be going into it with fear or worrying about what may happen, it’s more about what you want to go and achieve, whilst also accepting if it happens you can deal with it.”

A report relayed by The Peoples Person revealed Manchester United have planned to offer their full support to Lammens, imploring the team’s leadership group, which includes Heaton, to “reach out to him to show their support.”

But Heaton’s assured stance on the mental resilience of his younger teammate is promising – and is backed up by United legend Gary Pallister, who in an exclusive interview with our commander-in-chief, Red Billy, claimed Lammens is “strong enough to put that behind him”.

Feature image Orlando Ramirez via Getty Images

The Peoples Person has been one of the world’s leading Man United news sites for over a decade. bsky.social

title: Blackhawks Still Have Big Move To Make After Huge Bedard Deal
description: The Blackhawks should not be done making moves yet.
fullText:
The Chicago Blackhawks made a major move on Saturday, signing superstar center Connor Bedard to a five-year contract with an average annual value of $15 million. With this, the Blackhawks have locked up their top star through the 2030-31 season.

Getting Bedard signed to his new deal was the biggest objective the Blackhawks had this off-season. While they have now successfully done that, they have another important move to make before the season starts: adding another top-six winger.

There is no question that the Blackhawks could use a proven star in their top six as they look to take a step forward in 2026-27. They already made one notable addition when they acquired defenseman Bowen Byram from the Buffalo Sabres, but they should not be done adding to their group yet.

It would be significant if the Blackhawks could add a star winger to their roster before the season starts. Yet, even if they simply added a second-line caliber winger to their group, they would look more competitive in 2026-27.

It will be interesting to see if the Blackhawks can improve their forward group before the start of the season. If not, they should look to add another winger during the season.

title: England player ratings for entire World Cup: Wasted inclusion gets 2/10 but one star fetches 9/10
description: A ‘what could have been’ tournament brought England semi-final heartbreak versus Argentina, then a thrilling win over France for bronze medals
fullText:
Another World Cup is in the books, and you know what that means: four more years of hurt, minimum, in England’s bid to replicate the triumph of 1966.

The Three Lions arrived in North America with a 26-man squad and a German coach in Thomas Tuchel, who led the England side at a major tournament for the first time. And on the face of it, a semi-final run (just the fourth in England’s World Cup history) was an admirable effort, with no shame in losing to reigning champions Argentina.

Yet the late heartbreak in that semi-final was frustratingly familiar, with Tuchel under fire for his defensive approach in the final half-hour. In fact, for some fans, the defeat has re-contextualised Tuchel’s own tournament: did he bring the right players, and did he deploy his selected group properly?

There were zero minutes for Kobbie Mainoo, while Jordan Henderson’s divisive inclusion gave way to a broken wrist as an unused substitute in a farcical scene. Then there was striker and captain Harry Kane, who scored another healthy helping of goals but is running out of competitions to win gold with England.

Below, we rate every England player at the 2026 World Cup, after the Three Lions signed off with a thrilling 6-4 win over France in the bronze final.

Jordan Pickford – 7/10: No howlers, but not the clean-sheet assuredness we’ve seen in tournaments gone by. In fact, Pickford leaked at least a goal in five of his seven matches and could have done better for DR Congo’s goal and Enzo Fernandez’s equaliser for Argentina. That being said, his performance against Mexico was one of his best, if not his very best, in an England shirt.

Dean Henderson – 5: How about that for a World Cup debut? The Palace stopper was handed his chance in the third-place play-off but conceded four goals to a rampant France in the second-half. In his defence, every goal was a sumptuous finish.

James Trafford – N/A: Didn’t feature at all and, deep down, probably a bit miffed he didn’t get the nod over Henderson on Saturday. However, hard to argue when you’ve been a No 2 all season. He’ll be targeting the No 1 jersey for Euro 2028 but, first, he’ll need to find a No 1 jersey in the Premier League.

Reece James – 6: Such a figure of frustration. When fit and available, his quality is unquestionable, both on the ball with his passing (as shown in his midfield stints) and off the ball with his strong defensive efforts. However, a hamstring issue reared its head and he missed three of England’s eight matches at a crucial time of the tournament.

Jarell Quansah – 3: Tough tournament debut. Trusted at right-back in James’ absence against Mexico, he looked assured until lunging in and receiving a red card. His subsequent two-game ban was a big blow for Tuchel amid a crisis at right-back. And, in hindsight, it was a completely needless lunge-in.

Djed Spence – 7: What a topsy-turvy tournament for the Tottenham defender. Down in the doldrums after a poor display against DR Congo, Spence looked brilliant as a substitute against Mexico and Norway before that starry-eyed display versus Argentina – and that tackle. His pacey recoveries bring back memories of a certain Kyle Walker.

Ezri Konsa – 6: A starter in every match apart from the semi-final against Argentina shows the trust Tuchel placed in the versatile Aston Villa defender. Grew into the tournament after a nervy start against Croatia and deserved his goal against France. Could he have been a bit sharper on an unmarked Lautaro Martinez in stoppage time against Argentina? Perhaps.

John Stones – 6: Looked really rusty against Croatia but, again, grew into the tournament as he played more. A real leader in that England dressing room too, with 94 caps to his name. And we’ll always have that “shoulder injury” dance video….

Trevoh Chalobah: – N/A: Came on in the 93rd minute against France for his only minutes of the tournament. A somewhat odd replacement for wing-back Tino Livramento, it never looked likely that Chalobah would be properly called upon.

Marc Guehi – 7: Oddly dropped for the opener, Guehi then featured from the Ghana game onwards and grabbed the mantle of England’s most accomplished defender. In particular, his nullifying of Man City teammate Erling Haaland set England on their way to the semis.

Dan Burn – 7: A cult figure amongst the fans, Burn’s substitute appearances against Mexico and Norway were iconic, forcefully winning headers and showing a level of passion to make any Englishman proud. And yet, substituting him on against Argentina just gave Lionel Messi and Co even more impetus at the end of the semi-final.

Nico O’Reilly – 6: Dropped for the semi-final, O’Reilly was simply solid throughout. Never looked overawed by any occasion in his first tournament and built up a good understanding with Anthony Gordon on the left. Is it too harsh to say we expected a smidge more going forward given his Man City attacking exploits?

Elliot Anderson – 7: His first tournament with England and a promising start. Was not perfect, but his energy was something to admire, and there were touches of technical quality that were overlooked by many observers. A partnership with Rice would be trusty at future competitions, but is a different profile of midfielder needed (too?).

Declan Rice – 7: Struggled for full fitness as the tournament wore on, preventing him from being at his best, when England really needed him to be. However, he keeps a pretty strong rating here for his right-back stint against DR Congo and that impressive opening goal versus France.

Jordan Henderson – 2: Had the briefest cameo in a nothing spell of the game against Panama, then broke his wrist as an unused substitute against Mexico. Yes, all sympathy to him for that, and without going behind closed doors, we can’t speak to the value of his experience and leadership... but his place this summer was ultimately wasted on him.

Kobbie Mainoo – N/A: Didn’t play a single minute, raising questions over why he was brought at all. Bronze final versus France seemed a surefire moment for him to feature... only for a back injury to rule him out. Could his ability to hold the ball have helped against Argentina? We’ll never know.

Jude Bellingham – 9: Stepped up on so many occasions, showing once again that he is the future of this team – and already more important than Kane, whose six goals he eclipsed with an all-time, English record of seven at a major tournament. His efforts were ceaseless, although that petty streak needs watching...

Morgan Rogers – 6: Showed his quality with that assist for Gordon in the semi-final, which – in another world – could have taken on huge historical importance for England. His initiative to shoot from range against Norway also led to a crucial Bellingham rebound, but these moments were the extent of his contributions, as the ‘Rogers v Bellingham’ No 10 battle never got going.

Bukayo Saka – 7: Boosted his rating with that hat-trick in the thrilling 6-4 win over France, having started the tournament well with three assists to his name. However, in the heart of England’s run, he failed to make his usual mark. That was clearly down to injury struggles, though, which at times begged the question of whether he should have even been brought to the World Cup.

Eberechi Eze – 5: Did well against France after getting his first start of the tournament. Before that, he did not impress as a substitute, but also wasn’t given too many chances to, given Gordon grew into the competition out left and Bellingham was exceptional through the middle.

Noni Madueke – 5: There’s a temptation to go lower here, with the Arsenal winger having been actively poor against Norway, a performance that still sticks out. His tournament peaked in the opener, as he won a spot-kick versus Croatia. However, he did work hard all summer, having been tasked with more minutes than one would’ve expected, due to Saka’s injury issues.

Harry Kane – 7: Given the captain notched another six World Cup goals, plus a crucial assist against Mexico, this rating may feel harsh. After all, he was the hero with a double against DR Congo, crucial with a brace against Croatia, and his penalty versus Mexico also proved essential. But Kane could not make an impact in the quarter-final versus Norway or the semi-final versus Argentina, and those misfires in the biggest moments cost him slightly here.

Ivan Toney – 3: A reflection of his lack of game time, not a lack of quality per se. He was barely given a few minutes at the end of the Argentina tie, and although he started against France, he was unable to get in on the many, many goals.

Ollie Watkins – 3: Again, a rating based on a lack of game time. Had a handful of minutes prior to the second half against France in the bronze final. Surely should have been given more opportunities to show his worth.

Marcus Rashford – 5: That clinical finish against Croatia, in England’s opener, promised so much. Rashford was a revelation off the bench in that game, but he failed to replicate that showing against Ghana, and both his minutes and impact dwindled as the tournament went on.

Anthony Gordon – 7: Deserves immense credit for turning his tournament around, having produced a couple of early performances that rightfully cost him his place in the starting XI. He won a penalty against Mexico and improved further to score against Argentina, a moment he should cherish as much as possible.

title: NASCAR lineup for Sunday race at North Wilkesboro. Ryan Blaney gets the nod
description: Sunday's race will be the first points-paying, regular-season Cup Series race at North Wilkesboro since NASCAR left there 30 years ago.
fullText:
Ryan Blaney, fresh off a win last week at Atlanta last weekend, gets to start first Sunday night in the Window World 450 at North Wilkesboro Speedway in North Carolina.

And he didn't even have to mash the gas.

With Saturday's qualifying rained out, NASCAR turned to its fallback system and set the Sunday lineup using what it calls "metrics," which are weighed by last week's finish (70%) and season-long points (30%). Blaney sits third in points, and going along with last week's win, he's King of the Metrics! For this week, anyway.

He'll be joined on the front row by Ty Gibbs. Current points leader Denny Hamlin starts seventh while second-place Tyler Reddick starts fifth.

TV coverage begins at 7 p.m. on TNT.

1. Ryan Blaney, No. 12 Team Penske Ford

2. Ty Gibbs, No. 54 Joe Gibbs Racing Toyota

3. Christopher Bell, No. 20 Joe Gibbs Racing Toyota

4. Carson Hocevar, No. 77 Spire Motorsports Chevrolet

5. Tyler Reddick, No. 45 23XI Racing Toyota

6. Erik Jones, No. 43 Legacy Motor Club Toyota

7. Denny Hamlin, No. 11 Joe Gibbs Racing Toyota

8. Shane van Gisbergen, No. 97 Trackhouse Racing Chevrolet

9. Chris Buescher, No. 17 RFK Racing Ford

10. Chase Elliott, No. 9 Hendrick Motorsports Chevrolet

11. Joey Logano, No. 22 Team Penske Ford

12. Austin Dillon, No. 3 Richard Childress Racing Chevrolet

13. Ross Chastain, No. 1 Trackhouse Racing Chevrolet

14. Austin Cindric, No. 2 Team Penske Ford

15. William Byron, No. 24 Hendrick Motorsports Chevrolet

16. Michael McDowell, No. 71 Spire Motorsports Chevrolet

17. Daniel Suarez, No. 7 Spire Motorsports Chevrolet

18. Todd Gilliland, No. 34 Front Row Motorsports Ford

19. John Hunter Nemechek, No. 42 Legacy Motor Club Toyota

20. Cole Custer, No. 41 Haas Factory Team Chevrolet

21. Ryan Preece, No. 60 RFK Racing Ford

22. Ty Dillon, No. 10 Kaulig Racing Chevrolet

23. Brad Keselowski, No. 6 RFK Racing Ford

24. Ricky Stenhouse Jr., No. 47 HYAK Motorsports Chevrolet

25. Alex Bowman, No. 48 Hendrick Motorsports Chevrolet

26. Bubba Wallace, No. 23 23XI Racing Toyota

27. Kyle Larson, No. 5 Hendrick Motorsports Chevrolet

28. Josh Berry, No. 21 Wood Brothers Racing Ford

29. Zane Smith, No. 38 Front Row Motorsports Ford

30. Chase Briscoe, No. 19 Joe Gibbs Racing Toyota

31. Noah Gragson, No. 4 Front Row Motorsports Ford

32. Connor Zilisch, No. 88 Trackhouse Racing Chevrolet

33. Austin Hill, No. 33 Richard Childress Racing Chevrolet

34. Riley Herbst, No. 35 23XI Racing Toyota

35. AJ Allmendinger, No. 16 Kaulig Racing Chevrolet

36. Cody Ware, No. 51 Rick Ware Racing Chevrolet

37. Chad Finchum, No. 66 Garage 66 Ford

title: Report – Lazio Contemplate Summer Swoop for Milan Flop
description: Milan outcast Yunus Musah could reportedly join Lazio on a season-long loan with an option to buy after being cast out by Ruben Amorim. According to Calciomercato, the Biancocelesti are weighing up a ...
fullText:
Milan outcast Yunus Musah could reportedly join Lazio on a season-long loan with an option to buy after being cast out by Ruben Amorim. According to Calciomercato, the Biancocelesti are weighing up a formal offer for the United States international.

The 23-year-old spent last season on loan at Atalanta. However, after failing to secure regular first-team football in Bergamo, La Dea refused to trigger the buyout clause. As such, Musah has returned to San Siro, but Milan are already looking to send him elsewhere.

He made only five Serie A starts last season, and it’s unsurprising that Mauricio Pochettino omitted him from his World Cup squad. Keen to revive his faltering career, Musah, who has fallen out of favor at Milan, is eager to start a new chapter, and Lazio could come to his rescue.

With two years left to run on his contract, Milan could still recoup the €18 million they paid Valencia to sign Musah in 2023. Unfortunately, Musah’s skills are not exactly in high demand at the moment. Therefore, another loan deal appears far more likely.

Lazio may be willing to shell out €2 million to secure the USMNT midfielder’s services for a year. The proposed deal would include a purchase option worth around €18 million, which would automatically become an obligation if they qualify for European competition.

It’s understood that new Lazio boss Gennaro Gattuso keeps Musah in high esteem. His long-standing ties with Milan could also help facilitate negotiations, especially considering the Rossoneri are unwilling to stand in the player’s way.

title: Clairey Lin, 16-year-old from Canada, wins U.S. Girls' Junior
description: Clairey Lin of Canada won the U.S. Girls’ Junior championship on Saturday, beating Amelie Zalsman of St. Petersburg, Florida, 5 and 4.
fullText:
DURHAM, N.C. — Clairey Lin of Canada won the U.S. Girls’ Junior championship on Saturday, beating Amelie Zalsman of St. Petersburg, Florida, 5 and 4 in the 36-hole final at Old Chatham Golf Club.

The 16-year-old Lin, the stroke-play medalist, is the second straight Canadian winner. Aphrodite Deng won last year in Georgia.

“I’ve always wanted to be on the big stage and inspire some people and work hard and see it pay off,” Lin said. “To be able to do that today is amazing.”

The 17-year-old Zalsman is set to play for Wake Forest in 2027.

“I’ve played so much golf the past two weeks,” Zalsman said. “I’m just really proud of myself for making it this far and my body, honestly, just sticking with me.”

Deng skipped her title defense to play in the Evian Championship last week in France.

title: This is Spain's World Cup final, no matter who wins. Its DNA runs through both teams.
description: From Lionel Messi to Lamine Yamal, Barcelona and Spanish soccer helped shape the stars, styles and ideas that define both finalists.
fullText:
NEW YORK — It is an unthinkable image, impossible to imagine. Lionel Messi scores the winning goal of the World Cup final, running off and kissing the badge of Spain as his teammates surround him in a sea of red shirts.

Unthinkable, but not impossible. After the Rosario native got his start at tiny youth club Abanderado Grandoli and later joined the setup of Newell's Old Boys, he soon moved to FC Barcelona, eventually acquiring Spanish citizenship and becoming eligible for selection by La Roja.

How close was it to happening? Accounts vary. "We tried to get him to play with us ... but he never wanted to," said Ginés Meléndez, then director of Spain's youth national teams, during an interview with Spanish sports news publication "AS" this week.

What is certain is the Argentine federation scrambled together a U-20 friendly match so Messi would know he was wanted and ultimately put on the blue-and-white shirt at the top level.

But while Messi is as Argentine as asado, it's fair to say his soccer DNA is largely Spanish.

Barcelona helped pay for his growth hormone therapy, but also invested time and coaching into the 13-year-old who arrived in Catalonia before he made his pro debut there at 17 and not leaving until he was 34.

In fact, the DNA of top-level Spanish soccer, especially of FC Barcelona and its famed La Masia academy, courses through the World Cup final.

Most national teams are made up of players who have excelled in that country's domestic soccer. While eight members of Luis de la Fuente's Spain squad at this tournament now play outside powerful La Liga, all came through Spanish teams' academies.

Eight of them went through Barcelona, at the same club where Messi went through his development. The most notable is Lamine Yamal, the 19-year-old star who already helped Spain to the 2024 Euro title and now looks to add the World Cup. He may be the closest to a Messi-esque player Barcelona has been able to produce, even with De la Fuente saying Friday that Messi is an "unrepeatable" player.

Yamal is a crafty, left-footed attacker who burst onto the scene at an even younger age than Messi. Though clearly a different player than Messi, who was able to control the ball almost no matter the circumstances and often went on lengthy runs, Yamal's ability to both score and assist — plus his skill shown at a young age — may make him the best Barca player developed at the club since Messi.

Winning a World Cup would only add to his long list of achievements before the age of 20.

But it isn't just Yamal where Barcelona's influence will be felt. Pau Cubarsí is also 19 and has helped Barca to multiple league titles. A center back, his influence isn't as flashy but the work he puts in next to Aymeric Laporte was critical for Spain in the semifinal win over France as it kept a clean sheet for the sixth time this tournament.

Gavi, the untiring midfielder hailed as successor to Xavi, started at Real Betis but moved to La Masia before he made his professional debut. Dani Olmo, Marc Cucurella and Eric García are among the others who developed with Barcelona.

Argentina, too, has deeper roots in Spain than simply Messi's La Masia upbringing. Six players on its squad currently play in Spain. Veterans Rodrigo De Paul and Nicolás Tagliafico, for example, got their first taste of soccer outside Argentina with moves to Spain.

The DNA in this game speaks to Spain's culture of soccer development, of small teams in all corners of a country made up of very distinct climates and even languages putting an emphasis on coaching education, on developing great players and then sending them to larger clubs in the region for further development.

That development doesn't stop at the top level. Perhaps because of the dominance of Barcelona and Real Madrid, almost every other team in the country must think about how it can make the money needed to keep going at the top level. Most have a plan that involves selling top prospects to Barca, Madrid, clubs in the Premier League or another league abroad.

While it was a Spanish club that developed Messi, Argentines also have formed part of Spain's soccer history. Beyond Messi, Diego Maradona, Alfredo Di Stefano and Javier Mascherano, who left their marks on La Liga, the most influential Argentine has been Diego Simeone.

The former Atlético Madrid midfielder has now managed the club since 2011, leading it into a golden era with a distinct style of soccer that borrows from the "potrero," the dirt fields around the country where many Argentines first play. Disciplined but physical, Simeone sets up his team the way he thinks will best disrupt the opponent and give his side the best chance to win.

Now, one of Simeone's sons — 23-year-old Giuliano Simeone — is on the Argentina national team, helping them past England in the semifinal and hoping for another start in the final.

Yet, Spain also has been influenced by Simeone. Creative Villarreal product Álex Baena elevated his play after moving to Simeone at Atletico Madrid, and Marcos Llorente is a fixture for Simeone as one of the poster boys for how the team plays.

Lionel Scaloni, Argentina's manager, is another influenced by Spanish soccer, playing 200 matches with Deportivo La Coruña, while also seeing his ideas and players influence Spanish club play.

"I have an extraordinary admiration for a national team that was Copa América champion, World Cup, Copa América champion again, won the Finalissima, now a World Cup finalist," De la Fuente said Friday. "I don't think anyone has done that in history. And managed by a friend of mine. It’s admiration, admiration and more admiration, and recognition. They're great players."

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: This is Spain's World Cup final, no matter who wins. Its DNA runs through both teams.

title: Schalke reportedly working on next transfer coup
description: According to journalist Sascha Tavolieri, Schalke are working on a deal to bring defender Felix Uduokhai back to the Bundesliga. The report claims the newly promoted side have already opened talks wit...
fullText:
According to journalist Sascha Tavolieri, Schalke are working on a deal to bring defender Felix Uduokhai back to the Bundesliga. The report claims the newly promoted side have already opened talks with Beşiktaş over a potential transfer.

Schalke are hoping to secure the 27-year-old on an initial loan deal with an option to buy, while Uduokhai himself is said to be keen on making the move to Gelsenkirchen.

The centre-back remains under contract with Beşiktaş until 2027. After spending the 2024/25 season on loan from FC Augsburg, the Turkish club exercised their option to make the deal permanent last summer for a reported fee of €5 million.

Since arriving in Istanbul, Uduokhai has made 58 competitive appearances for Beşiktaş. Should the move go through, the former Germany youth international would become Schalke’s fifth signing of the summer, following the arrivals of Kevin Müller, Satoshi Tanaka, Junior Adamu and Robin Gosens.

title: Georgia football’s biggest lingering question ahead of 2026 season
description: ESPN analyst David Hale questions if the Georgia Bulldogs have enough talent at wide receiver for the 2026 season.
fullText:
The Georgia Bulldogs will enter the 2026 season as one of the top teams in college football. The Dawgs’ roster features a dynamic mix of young and veteran players, and the team as a whole will look to build off an excellent 2025 season moving forward.

Georgia’s defense will feature an elite group of stars both up front and in the secondary this season. Additionally, the Dawgs’ offense should quickly begin to shine despite Zachariah Branch’s departure this offseason. A handful of analysts around the college football world believe Georgia’s wide receiver corps as a whole could be a cause for concern entering 2026, however.

ESPN’s David Hale recently named Georgia football’s top lingering question prior to kickoff of the 2026 season, and the analyst asks whether Georgia’s receiving corps will be up to task.

“Let's be honest: Georgia's receiving talent hasn't been elite since Brock Bowers and Ladd McConkey departed after the 2023 season.” He wrote. “Yes, Zachariah Branch became a real weapon last year, but he did the bulk of his damage after the catch, and that often limited Georgia's downfield attack. Even after last season's mediocre results, however, there are potentially more questions this season with Colbie Young, Dillon Bell, Noah Thomas and tight end Oscar Delp gone. The Bulldogs added a solid transfer in Isiah Canion from Georgia Tech, but beyond that, the biggest solutions are likely to come from unproven players, including Talyn Taylor and CJ Wiley, along with a host of true freshmen from a strong signing class.”

Branch was a superstar throughout his lone season at Georgia. The Bulldogs will turn to former Georgia Tech Yellow Jackets’ star Isiah Canion as a top receiving option in hopes of replicating Branch’s production. Promising route runners Talyn Taylor and CJ Wiley will undoubtedly play a much larger role this season, and the duo will likely compete with Canion for targets as the season progresses.

Hale’s comments in regard to Georgia’s wide receiver corps may not come as a surprise to many. The group as a whole is unproven outside of Canion, but Taylor and Wiley can emerge as dangerous weapons over the course of 2026.

Follow UGA Wire on Instagram, Facebook, X (formerly Twitter) or Threads for more Georgia football coverage!

This article originally appeared on UGA Wire: Georgia football’s biggest lingering question ahead of 2026 season

title: NFL free agent market still has big names if Browns want to add before training camp
description: Could the Cleveland Browns roster see a veteran addition or 2? NFL free agency still has some options
fullText:
Given the team’s youth movement, the Cleveland Browns roster is mostly set heading into the start of training camp. The bigger questions are whether stars like CB Denzel Ward and S Grant Delpit will be extended or traded this season. Our looks at the Browns different depth charts show a lack of veteran names Cleveland fans are used to seeing:

We followed that up with the defensive side of the ball:

What if GM Andrew Berry and HC Todd Monken decide that the team needs to add some veteran talent to help the roster in 2026? NFL free agency has been going on for months, but really never ends. There are a lot of interesting, big names still available to be signed if Berry and Monken want to try to infuse experience into a young roster:

DE Jadeveon Clowney (highly doubtful)

Almost everyone wants a Chubb return.

With the departure of Myles Garrett, Bosa and Reddick could get the most interest from the Browns. The remade offensive line still needs to put together who will play center and right guard, which could open up a return for Zeitler, while Wagner’s leadership and experience could be helpful on the defensive side of the ball.

Given the young talent at wide receiver, it seems unlikely one of the big veteran names on the list will be added, but Berry and Monken could see a need after offseason activities.

For Cleveland to add a big-name veteran in free agency at the start of training camp, one of three things would likely be true:

Young player(s) are not ready to contribute

GM and HC believe the roster is ready to compete for the AFC North this year

Two of the three would be negative reasons, but the third would be very positive.

Of the names listed above, which one do you think the Browns should sign? Do you think Berry should be looking for additional veterans or stick with the young roster?

Join the conversation in the comment section AND vote in DBN’s Feed poll

title: More Bad News for Aaron Judge, Yankees
description: The New York Yankees are missing their biggest star right now and that won't change any time soon.
fullText:
The New York Yankees are missing their biggest star in Aaron Judge and made a roster move involving him on Saturday.

On Saturday, the Yankees officially moved Judge to the 60-Day Injured List as he continued to recover from his fractured rib. Now, of course, this doesn't add 60 days to his total amount of time he needs to stay on the Injured List, in large part because he already has missed so much time. But it is yet another example of him not being close to a return.

Judge, himself, gave an update on his rib earlier in the week.

"Some progress, some healing going on, but not fully healed," Judge said. "So we're still waiting on one more doctor to take a look at it, kind of see how we progress forward the next couple of weeks. But definitely a positive sign that we're seeing some healing.”

Looking for the perfect gift for a baseball player in your life? Check out our brand new baseball lifestyle brand!

Unfortunately for the Yankees, it doesn't sound like Judge is coming to save the day any time soon.

Do you have a take to get off your chest? Do you want to engage in a growing Yankees community? Comment on our content alongside staff and fans alike!

New York is still in second place in the American League East, but for how long? The Boston Red Sox are coming.

More MLB: Could Red Sox Actually Pull Off Once Unthinkable Feat To End Season?

title: Patriots 53-man roster projection ahead of training camp | Karen Guregian
description: Here's an early roster projection with padded practice sessions and joint practices with Indy and Philadelphia still to come.
fullText:
Mike Vrabel is set to kick off his second training camp at the helm of the Patriots.

Through the course of the next five weeks, Vrabel and his coaching staff will have seen enough to whittle the roster down to the necessary 53.

Before seeing more full-fledged action with padded sessions and joint practices with both Indianapolis and Philadelphia, here’s my take on who makes it, and who doesn’t after the spring camps and minicamp.

IN - Tommy DeVito, Drake Maye, Behren Morton

While it’s hard imagining that Maye will top some of his outrageous statistical marks from last season, most notably completion percentage, it’s not crazy to think he’ll actually be better.

Now being in his second year in Josh McDaniels offense, having a better receiving cast, and improved blocking, Maye figures to improve.

DeVito and Morton are simply along for the ride.

IN - TreVeyon Henderson, Lan Larison, Rhamondre Stevenson

OUT - Terrell Jennings, Jam Miller, Myles Montgomery

The competition for the third back behind Stevenson and Henderson will be one of the better position battles during training camp.

Heading in, Larison has a slight edge based on his work in the spring, and how he was used. He looked good last year before getting hurt.

But there’s still plenty of time for Jennings, Miller and or Montgomery to win the job.

The Patriots had an eye on improving the run game, making it more consistent by wanting a legitimate fullback at Josh McDaniels disposal.

Gilliam will open holes and lead the way for running backs. He might also surprise some people catching the ball out of the backfield.

IN - A.J. Brown, Romeo Doubs, Efton Chism, DeMario Douglas, Mack Hollins, Kyle Williams

OUT - Kayshon Boutte, Nick, DeGennaro, Kyle Dixon, Cameron Dorner, Jimmy Kibble, Jeremiah Webb

I’ve written that the Patriots should keep Boutte given his connection with Maye. But considering how minicamp played out, with Boutte not participating in team drills the final day, he looks to be the odd man out.

Brown, meanwhile looked terrific, and while Doubs didn’t necessarily stand out, he did enough to whet the appetite toward the possibilities with him riding shotgun with Brown.

IN - Hunter Henry, Eli Raridon, C.J. Dippre

OUT - Tanner Arkin, Jack Westover

It’s Henry, Raridon, and see who emerges, or who else the Patriots bring in.

Julian Hill’s season-ending injury leaves the Patriots thin in the No. 2 hole. Raridon made some plays but doesn’t seem ready to assume that job out of the gate.

Dippre is the placeholder for now over Westover, and undrafted rookie Arkin, who is someone to watch during training camp given his ability to block.

IN - Ben Brown (G-C), Will Campbell (T), Dametrious Crownover (T), Caleb Lomu (T), Morgan Moses (T), Mike Onwenu (G) Andrew Rupcich (G), Alijah Vera-Tucker (G), Jared Wilson (C-G)

OUT - Marcus Bryant (T), Mehki Butler (G), Sebastian Gutierrez (T), James Hudson III (T), Lorenz Metz (T), JonDarius Morgan (G), Jacob Rizy (C), Caedan Wallace (T/G)

Rookie Lomu looks like a safe bet as the swing tackle. For now, keeping Crownover over Bryant. Depending on Morgan Moses’ health, the Patriots might have to keep both along with Lomu.

As for guard, Rupcich makes it over Wallace this time out.

IN - Christian Barmore, Cory Durden, Joshua Farmer, Leonard Taylor III, Milton Williams

OUT - David Blay, Jr., Eric Gregory, Jeremiah Pharms, Travis Shaw

Even without Khyiris Tonga, the Patriots still seem pretty strong up front in the middle.

Taylor came on toward the end of the season, and looked good in the early camps.

Gregory and or Pharms could land on the final 53, but Taylor and Joshua Farmer look like the best bets to back up Barmore, Durden and Williams at this stage.

IN - Gabe Jacas, Dre-Mont Jones, Harold Landry III, Christian Ponder, Bradyn Swinson

OUT - Xavier Holmes, Quintayvious Hutchins, Jesse Luketa

The question marks outweigh and overshadow the positives with this group.

Rookie second-round pick Jacas missed all of the early camps having not signed his contract while also recovering from a “procedure,” reportedly a knee clean-out.

Vrabel & Co. were high on him during the draft. But his status is uncertain going forward.

Landry had off-season knee surgery. The Patriots are taking it slow with him.

Jones, signed in free agency, has drawn praise from the coaching staff. Ditto Ponder and Swinson.

Holmes, an undrafted rookie, stood out to position coach Mike Smith in the early camps. Probably not enough to make the team at this point. He’s another in the practice squad picture.

IN - K.J. Britt, Christian Elliss, Chad Muma, Namdi Obiazor, Robert Spillane

OUT - Amari Gainer, Khalil Jacobs, Otis Reese

This group is basically Spillane, Elliss, and who fills in behind the duo.

Muma had the green dot at times during the early camps. Since being signed off the Colts practice squad in December last year, the Wyoming alum has gradually made his presence felt.

Britt was signed during the offseason and given his special teams ability, makes it. So does rookie sixth-round pick Obiazor.

If the Patriots carry four, it’ll probably come down to Britt or Muma getting the final spot.

IN - Mike Brown, Kevin Byard, Dell Pettus, Brenden Schooler, Craig Woodson

OUT - Peter Manuma, John Saunders Jr.

Like defensive line, another strong group led by the starting pair of Byard and Woodson.

Brown, who stood out in the early camps, and Pettus will be the primary backups along with Schooler, a core special teamer who occasionally appears with the defense.

IN - Carlton Davis III, Christian Gonzalez, Marcus Jones, Karon Prunty, Kindle Vildor

OUT - Channing Canada, Brandon Crossley, Marcellus Dial, Kenneth Harris, Charles Woods

After the big three of Gonzalez, Davis and Jones, the backups aren’t the easiest call.

Rookie fifth-round pick Prunty has shown enough, and has the type of traits that warrant keeping him around. So he gets one of the spots.

The other comes down to Woods, who played well when given the opportunity last year, and Vildor, who was signed during the offseason and played for Vrabel in Tennessee.

Vildor stood out in the early camps, moreso than Woods. That might flip during training camp. But for now, Vildor is in.

Dial, a 2024 sixth round pick, didn’t play last year due to suffering a torn ACL in August. He’s working his way back, but right now doesn’t make the cut.

IN - Julian Ashy (LS), Bryce Baringer (P), Andy Borregalas (K)

At different points, the Patriots cycled in a long snapper, punter and kicker during the early camps, but no one stuck around.

So it’s the same special teams trio from last year.

title: Fanatics Debuts “We’re Still Wearing It” Campaign Honoring Lionel Messi Ahead of World Cup Final
description: In what may be the Argentine legend's final game for his nation's national team, the sports company is debuting a film that declares “You Can’t Hang It Up While We’re Still Wearing It.”
fullText:
Hours before Lionel Messi plays in the 2026 FIFA World Cup Final, in what may be his final game on the Argentinian national team, the sports apparel, collectibles and entertainment company Fanatics is launching a campaign that focuses on Messi’s iconic number 10 jersey and declares “You Can’t Hang It Up While We’re Still Wearing It.”

Fanatics debuted a short film called “We’re Still Wearing It” Sunday morning, which features a Spanish-language version of the song “Don’t Cry For Me Argentina” by Luz Gaggi, a finalist on Argentina’s The Voice.

More from The Hollywood Reporter

Where to Watch France vs. England World Cup Third-Place Playoff Online

Disney's Next Big Product Line Sees Its Star Characters Collide With NFL Teams

The NBA Wants to Set Next Season's TV Schedule, But It's Waiting On LeBron

The film sees a giant Messi jersey being raised in his honor, a metaphor for his presumed retirement, before the citizens rally to tear it down and express their desire to see him continue to play.

Fanatics says that the film, which is set in Buenos Aires, was cast entirely with real fans of Messi, going so far as to source extras from an Argentinian fan group. It is also filled with Easter eggs that reference Messi and his career, including jerseys that span his entire run for the Argentinian national team.

Over the next two days, Fanatics will also erect “We’re Still Wearing It” billboards in a dozen cities from around the world, many with connections to Messi, including in Rosario, Argentina, where Messi was born; Barcelona and Paris, cities where he’s played; Lake Town, India, home to a 70-foot Messi statue. Other cities include New York, Buenos Aires, London, Mexico City, Los Angeles, Lagos, Tokyo and São Paulo.

Later today, Messi and Argentina will play in the World Cup final in New Jersey, facing off against Spain. While Messi will return to the MLS team Inter Miami after the World Cup is over, his future with the Argentinian national team is in doubt at 39 years old.

Fanatics hosted Messi at Fanatics Fest on Friday for the World Cup press conference, and the company’s Topps collectibles brand is sponsoring the World cup’s first ever halftime show, which will pack performances from BTS, Madonna, Shakira, Burna Boy, Justin Bieber and Gustavo Dudamel and his orchestra into what the producer calls an “action-packed 11 minutes.”
